summary: This satirical takedown dissects a Cadillac commercial that aired during the Olympics, exposing its celebration of American workaholism and wealth as toxic ideology. The author, writing as Leopold Dilg, follows the ad's 'Master of the Universe' protagonist as he sneers at European work-life balance while strutting through his mansion. Through biting commentary, the piece reveals how the commercial's 'you make your own luck' message ignores the millions of unemployed, foreclosed, and struggling Americans. The essay exposes the cruel irony of selling bootstrap mythology to a nation gutted by the very Wall Street class the ad glorifies.
key_points: The Cadillac ad uses nationalist pride and anti-European sentiment to glorify overwork as an American virtue
The 'you make your own luck' ideology conveniently ignores structural inequality and the millions harmed by economic policies favoring the wealthy
The commercial co-opts genuinely admirable figures like Muhammad Ali to legitimize plutocratic values they would likely reject
The ad's protagonist represents the Wall Street class whose excesses caused the very economic devastation that makes 'making your own luck' impossible for most
Using French phrases while mocking the French reveals the ad's incoherent cultural posturing
money_quotes: There's little chance we can underestimate our American virtues, since our overlords so seldom miss an opportunity to point them out.
Those French will stop at nothing.
Never in a million years would the hip jazz guitarist insist on taking an August holiday. And the imprisoned-for-draft-dodging boxer couldn't possibly side with the café-loafers on the WMD thing.
Damn, if only the NASA budget wasn't cut after all that looting by the Wall Street boys to pay for their $10 million vacation homes.
The 17 million unemployed and underemployed, the 47 million who need food stamps to keep from starving, the 8 million families thrown out of their homes – WE ALL BELIEVE.
You work hard, you create your own luck, and you gotta believe anything is possible.

From the 70-year olds doing Wal-mart inventory because their retirement went bust, from all the kitchens of all the families carrying $1 trillion in college debt, I hear the national chant, 'YOU MAKE YOUR OWN LUCK!'
key_insight: The 'you make your own luck' mythology serves to justify plutocratic excess while blaming victims of systemic economic injustice for their own suffering.
